title: Life-cycle air emissions from PV power systems
abstract:
Life-cycle Air Emissions from
PV Power Systems
M. E. Watt,1,* A. J. Johnson,1 M. Ellis2 and H. R. Outhred1
1Photovoltaics Special Research Centre, School of Electrical Engineering, University of New South Wales, Sydney 2052,
Australia
231 Russell Street, Lily®eld, NSW 2040, Australia
This paper addresses the air emissions of grid supply versus grid-connected and
o-grid photovoltaic power generation, using the framework of life-cycle assessment,
in the context of rural household energy supply in Australia. Emissions of carbon
dioxide, sulphur dioxide and nitrous oxides are calculated for the three life-cycle
stages of manufacture, use and disposal. Sensitivities to materials and data inputs, as
well as to component eciencies, lifetimes and sizing are discussed.
For each supply option, demand management options, including insulation and
appliance choice, and the substitution of solar heating or bottled gas for electricity,
are considered.
The best option in all cases, in terms of life-cycle air emissions, is a grid-connected
photovoltaic system used to supply an energy-ecient household with a mix of solar,
gas and electric appliances. However, in ®nancial terms, with current Australian
energy prices, this option represents a high capital and life-cycle cost. Additionally,
for the grid options, electricity costs do not signi®cantly disadvantage the high
demand scenarios. Both results provide a clear illustration of current Australian
energy-pricing policies being in con¯ict with long-term environmental sustainability.
# 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
INTRODUCTION
T
his paper examines the air emissions of grid supply versus grid-connected and o-grid photo-
voltaic (PV) power generation, using the framework of life-cycle assessment (LCA), in the context
of rural household energy supply in Australia.1,2 Although only air emissions are examined in this
paper, LCA oers a holistic methodology for analysing the environmental burdens of an energy system
over its entire lifetime, from the manufacture of individual components to installation, use and ®nal
disposal, i.e. from cradle to grave.3±6 This represents a move beyond traditional cost±bene®t analysis into
a full exploration of the life-cycle consequences involved in the choice of systems to meet energy service
requirements.7,8
The aims of such analyses are to provide information to the public, the energy industry, governments
and utilities, which will assist the decision-making processes. In the Australian state of New South Wales
(NSW), for example, distribution wires businesses must now consider local generation and demand-side
options when grid extensions are undertaken in their service territories. In addition, electricity retailers
CCC 1062±7995/98/020127±10$17.50
Received 16 December 1997
# 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Revised 14 January 1998
PROGRESS IN PHOTOVOLTAICS: RESEARCH AND APPLICATIONS
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 6, 127±136 (1998)
Utrecht
Workshop
* Correspondence to: M. E. Watt, Photovoltaics Special Research Centre, School of Electrical Engineering, University of
New South Wales, Sydney 2052, Australia. E-mail: m.watt@unsw.edu.au
Contract/grant sponsor: Australian IEA PV Power Systems Consortium
 must prepare and report on greenhouse gas reduction strategies. Both requirements place the onus on the
electricity industry to show that its planning processes consider renewable energy options and environ-
mental impacts.
In this paper, the analysis of stand-alone, o-grid power generation is based on a PV±diesel hybrid
system sized for a typical Australian household. Demand management considerations, including insula-
tion and appliance choice, as well as the substitution of solar heating or bottled gas for electricity, intro-
duce variations to the analysis.
The air emissions of the PV system are compared with those from traditional rural grid extension and
grid-connected photovoltaics. Analysis of the grid supply option considers both transmission and distri-
bution network infrastructure and electricity generation, which in the Australian context is predominantly
coal-based.
SUPPLY-SIDE OPTIONS
The household modelled is a hypothetical but realistic one, with average energy requirements and situated
in an inland rural area 790 km north of Sydney, 220 km south of Brisbane (the closest major cities) and
20 km from the nearest town. Much of the materials, energy system components and appliances are
sourced from the cities, with building materials supplied locally.
The household is assumed to be 2 km from the nearest electricity grid,9 which is an 11-kV, three-phase
line that can be supplied by a stand-alone PV-based power system, a grid extension with a grid-connected
PV system or a standard grid extension. All are currently available options in Australia, with the second
option representing a PV grid support system for an existing grid connection that is nearing capacity
limits.
Option 1: PV stand-alone power supply system
A 2-kWp PV array10,11 is used in an o-grid hybrid power system. The total system includes 36 kWh
(roughly 1.5 days) of lead acid battery storage,12 with an in±out eciency of 85% and maximum depth of
discharge of 50%, a 3-kW inverter13 with an average eciency of 90% and a 5-kVA diesel generator.14,15
The PV array comprises multicrystalline silicon modules with aluminium frames, front glass and Tedlar
rear encapsulation. An annual average daily DC output of 5 kWh kWp71 is used, which is typical of PV
performance in many areas of Australia. This system is typical of the `hybrid' power systems used in rural
Australia, although for very low loads (less than 2 kWh day71) a diesel generator may not be included.
Option 2: grid-connected photovoltaics
A 2-kWp grid-connected PV array is installed on the roof of the house and connected to the mains supply
via a 3-kW inverter with an average eciency of 95%. This eciency is slightly higher than that used for
the stand-alone system, to re¯ect the use of more modern and higher eciency inverters typically used for
grid-connected installations. The base case does not include battery storage; however, sensitivities to the
addition of batteries have been modelled and are discussed. The house is connected to the central grid via
a 2-km single-phase, 11-kV grid extension using wood poles and with a 10-kVA pole-top transformer at
the house.16,17 Central grid generation is modelled on the Australian average,18±22 which is largely coal
based with minor use of hydro and natural gas. Network losses average 12%.
Option 3: grid connection
The same grid extension is used as for option 2, with no PV-related components. This could be seen as the
most common reference case.
# 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 6, 127±136 (1998)
128
M. E. WATT ET AL.

in an inland rural area 790 km north of Sydney, 220 km south of Brisbane (the closest major cities) and
20 km from the nearest town. Much of the materials, energy system components and appliances are
sourced from the cities, with building materials supplied locally.
The household is assumed to be 2 km from the nearest electricity grid,9 which is an 11-kV, three-phase
line that can be supplied by a stand-alone PV-based power system, a grid extension with a grid-connected
PV system or a standard grid extension. All are currently available options in Australia, with the second
option representing a PV grid support system for an existing grid connection that is nearing capacity
Option 1: PV stand-alone power supply system
A 2-kWp PV array10,11 is used in an o-grid hybrid power system. The total system includes 36 kWh
(roughly 1.5 days) of lead acid battery storage,12 with an in±out eciency of 85% and maximum depth of
discharge of 50%, a 3-kW inverter13 with an average eciency of 90% and a 5-kVA diesel generator.14,15
The PV array comprises multicrystalline silicon modules with aluminium frames, front glass and Tedlar
rear encapsulation. An annual average daily DC output of 5 kWh kWp71 is used, which is typical of PV
performance in many areas of Australia. This system is typical of the `hybrid' power systems used in rural
Australia, although for very low loads (less than 2 kWh day71) a diesel generator may not be included.
Option 2: grid-connected photovoltaics
A 2-kWp grid-connected PV array is installed on the roof of the house and connected to the mains supply
via a 3-kW inverter with an average eciency of 95%. This eciency is slightly higher than that used for
the stand-alone system, to re¯ect the use of more modern and higher eciency inverters typically used for
grid-connected installations. The base case does not include battery storage; however, sensitivities to the
addition of batteries have been modelled and are discussed. The house is connected to the central grid via
a 2-km single-phase, 11-kV grid extension using wood poles and with a 10-kVA pole-top transformer at
the house.16,17 Central grid generation is modelled on the Australian average,18±22 which is largely coal
based with minor use of hydro and natural gas. Network losses average 12%.
The same grid extension is used as for option 2, with no PV-related components. This could be seen as the
# 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 6, 127±136 (1998)
For each of the supply options described above, three dierent energy demand scenarios were assessed:
(i) High electricity use scenario: typical house with all-electric appliance use.
(ii) Medium electricity use scenario: energy-ecient house with solar water heater and ecient electric
(iii) Low electricity use scenario: energy-ecient house using electricity and gas, with low energy use
appliances and solar water heating.
The base case load modelled (high electricity use scenario) is based on the average 1994 NSW household
load, with breakdown of appliance use also based on recent customer surveys.23,24 The base case load is
25 kWh day71, which is high for a stand-alone system and unlikely to be provided entirely by electricity.
The medium and low electricity use scenarios are more representative of those serviced by stand-alone
systems. The medium scenario represents an energy reduction of 47% compared to the high scenario,
while the low scenario represents an electricity reduction of 66% and a total energy reduction of 40%, the
latter re¯ecting the typically lower eciencies of gas appliances.
In each scenario, the basic house type and occupancy characteristics are the same, as are levels of energy
services received by the household. A summary of the characteristics of the energy demand scenarios, each
of which applies to all energy supply options, is provided in Table I.
Only changes in material usage from the base case represented by the high electricity use scenario are
modelled, as we have assumed an existing dwelling. It should also be noted that in the medium and low
scenarios we have speci®cally not attempted to describe an ultralow-energy dwelling or one that requires
lifestyle changes by the inhabitants. Energy use could be reduced further by such measures and by the
adoption of passive solar house design principles.
The energy source contributions to the load for the o-Grid, PV/grid and grid systems with high,
medium and low electricity use are shown in Figure 1.
Table I. Characteristics of energy demand scenarios
management (medium electricity
Reduced load due to insulation
Reduced load due to insulation
Reduced load due to insulation
Reduced load due to insulation
# 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 6, 127±136 (1998)
LIFE-CYCLE AIR EMISSIONS FROM PV SYSTEMS
Australia does not yet have extensive life-cycle databases available for general use. While some Australian
data are available on energy sources,25,26 much of the data used in this study were based on analyses
undertaken in other countries.27,28 Inputs were included for batteries, grid extension and grid electricity,
diesel generators and fuel, PV modules, inverters and bottled gas, as well as for the additional inputs
required for house insulation, ecient appliances and solar water heaters.
For each of the above components, information was compiled on carbon dioxide, sulphur dioxide and
nitrous oxide emissions for the three stages of the life-cycle:
(i) Stage 1: raw material extraction, processing, manufacture, construction and installation.
(ii) Stage 2: operation and delivered energy use.
(iii) Stage 3: decommissioning.
The primary materials included in the assessment are:29±34
The total annual system CO2, NOx and SOx emissions are summarized in Figures 2, 4 and 5. The
eciencies and lifetime sensitivities tested for CO2 are shown in Table II and illustrated in Figure 3.
Uncertainties in the input data mean that the results are best viewed in terms of high, medium and low
bands, rather than drawing ®ne distinctions between individual numbers.
Figure 1. Energy source contribution to load for high, medium and low electricity use
# 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 6, 127±136 (1998)
As shown in Figure 2, CO2 emissions are highest, between 10,000 and 12,000 kg year71 for the o-grid
and grid supply options with high electricity demands. In both cases the use of fossil fuel-based electricity
generation produces the highest emissions.
Medium CO2 emissions between 4000 and 7000 kg year71, result from the o-grid system with medium
load, the PV/Grid system with high load and the grid system with medium or low electricity loads, with
fossil fuel use still dominating. For the o-grid system with low electricity load and the PV/grid system
with medium or low loads, CO2 emissions are below 3000 kg year71 and the contributions of the various
system elements are more balanced, although the inverter contribution is insigni®cant. Overall, grid-
connected PV gives the lowest CO2 emissions for each demand scenario.
aFirst value for stand-alone system, second for grid-connected PV.
bValues are 15, 18 and 20% with 50% through batteries.
Figure 2. Life-cycle CO2 emissions for the dierent energy supply/demand options, showing the contribution of each
# 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 6, 127±136 (1998)
LIFE-CYCLE AIR EMISSIONS FROM PV SYSTEMS
These rankings are robust to extreme CO2 values from dierent input material databases reported in the
literature, as well as to grid extension length, largely because, for this case study, emissions from stage 1 of
the life-cycle (manufacture, etc.) are swamped by emissions from stage 2 (operation and end-use) for all
but the o-grid and PV/grid options with low electricity demands.
However, there is some sensitivity in rankings to extremes of system component eciencies and life-
times, as shown in Figure 3. For example, if higher PV, inverter, battery and diesel eciencies are used,
combined with longer lifetimes for PV system and grid components, overall emissions for the o-grid
system are signi®cantly reduced. There is also sensitivity in the rankings to PV array size. If a 1-kW PV
array is used rather than a 2-kW array, the stand-alone option has the highest emissions for all demand
scenarios, and emissions from the grid-connected PV system increase signi®cantly.
Although not shown in Figure 3, the sensitivity of CO2 emissions to adding batteries to the PV/grid
system are very similar to those shown for using a 1-kW PV array. However, if battery size is doubled to
3 days of storage, emission levels for the PV/grid system are similar to those of the base case. For the o-
grid system, doubling the battery size results in emission levels slightly higher than the base case, but not
as high as for the 1-kW PV array.
For SO2 emissions, the grid supply option consistently has the highest emissions, re¯ecting the high coal
component of the Australian central grid generation mix. The lowest SO2 emissions are from the grid-
connected PV system with low electricity demand. These results are illustrated in Figure 4.
These results are robust to extremes of available material input data and to high and low compo-
nent eciencies and lifetimes. However, the o-grid supply option results in the lowest SO2 emissions
across all demand scenarios if batteries are added to the grid-connected PV system or if the PV array size is
Figure 3. Carbon dioxide sensitivities to eciencies and lifetimes and PV array size for dierent electricity supply
options with high, medium and low household electricity loads
# 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 6, 127±136 (1998)
The various NOx emissions are shown in Figure 5. The o-grid system has the highest NOx emissions,
largely due to the use of the back-up diesel generator. However, for the lowest electricity load the PV
supplies the majority of electricity used and NOx emissions are signi®cantly reduced. The combined PV/
grid gives the lowest NOx emissions for each demand scenario. These results are robust to extremes of
material input values, eciencies and lifetimes, as well as to changes in the PV array size or the addition of
batteries to the grid-connected PV array.
The best option in all cases, in terms of life-cycle air emissions, is a grid-connected PV system used to
supply an energy-ecient household with a mix of solar, gas and electric appliances. More detailed assess-
ment of this option shows that, with the reduced fossil fuel inputs for electricity that are possible with this
option, the emissions from bottled gas and grid extension become relatively more signi®cant, followed by
the emissions from PV and the demand-side options. The relative contributions to emissions from each
component for CO2, SO2 and NOx for the low electricity load PV/grid option are shown in Figure 6.
Figure 4. Life-cycle SO2 emissions for the dierent energy supply options and demand scenarios
Figure 5. Life-cycle NOx emissions of the dierent energy supply options and demand scenarios
# 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 6, 127±136 (1998)
LIFE-CYCLE AIR EMISSIONS FROM PV SYSTEMS
Because most energy decisions are still made on the basis of economic cost eectiveness, we have under-
taken life-cycle cost comparisons of the various options using current Australian costs. Table III shows
the initial capital costs and the 20-year net present costs (in Australian dollars) of the dierent models,
expressed as a tari equivalent for the supply of energy services.
It is noteworthy that, although demand reduction leads to signi®cant emission reductions and reduces
annual energy expenditure, these bene®ts are not re¯ected in the levelized unit NPC, with the high demand
scenarios showing the lowest cost for each supply option. This is due to the reduced quantity of energy
supplied in comparison to the high demand scenarios, combined with the relatively low cost of diesel fuel,
gas and grid electricity, compared with the costs of the PV and demand management system elements.
For the grid options, a 2-km single phase extension is priced at around $21,200, with electricity to
rural customers priced at an average of around $0.12 kWh71. Expenditure on demand management
is compensated for by reduced electricity use, so that the annual average expenditure on energy is about
$3600 for all demand scenarios. It should be noted that a higher grid extension cost, resulting from tree
Figure 6. Relative contributions to emissions of the various system elements for the PV/grid supply option, with
Table III. Summary of net present cost outcomes (in Australian dollars)
aPV, $8 Wp71; inverter $4500; diesel, $9500; battery, $16,000; additional cost of energy-ecient appliances: medium scenario,
bIncluding capital, operation and maintenance and fuel costs and using 7% discount rate, 60 c l71 diesel fuel, 1.5 c MJ71 gas and
12.55 c kWh71 grid electricity.
# 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Prog. Photovolt. Res. Appl. 6, 127±136 (1998)
clearing, dicult terrain or a greater distance, would make the demand management options even more
When a PV system is added to the grid, the capital cost increases to $41,860 and annual average energy
expenditure is $5850, decreasing slightly with reduced electricity demand. The o-grid option has the
highest annual average costs but shows signi®cant reductions with reduced electricity demand.
It is clear that CO2 emissions are orders of magnitude larger than SO2 or NOx emissions, although the
relative impacts are incommensurate. It is also clear that the high energy demand scenario consistently has
the highest air emissions, re¯ecting the dominance of stage 2 of the life-cycle: operation and delivered
energy use. This also shows that demand-side options to reduce total energy use and to substitute for fossil
fuel-generated electricity have at least as signi®cant an impact on emissions as do the energy supply
options. Should other impacts such as land, water and scarce material use or ecotoxicity be considered, the
rankings of the dierent options may change.
The best option in all cases, in terms of life-cycle air emissions, is a grid-connected PV system used to
supply an energy-ecient household with a mix of solar, gas and electric appliances. The most signi®cant
emissions for this option are from the use of bottled gas and from the grid extension. However, in ®nancial
terms, with current Australian energy prices, this combination of grid extension, PV array, solar water
heater and energy-ecient appliances represents a high capital cost and a life-cycle cost 50% higher than
for a standard grid connection. Additionally, for the grid options, electricity costs do not signi®cantly
disadvantage the high demand scenarios. Both results provide a clear illustration of current Australian
energy-pricing policies being in con¯ict with long-term environmental sustainability.
Finally, it is worth noting the general lack of data necessary to undertake detailed life-cycle analyses,
because many of the most comprehensive databases are maintained in commercial con®dence due to
private sector funding. There is also little consistency in the available data and the approach in data
gathering is often undocumented. This means that life-cycle assessment cannot yet be used to its full
potential to provide an environmentally sensitive adjunct to economic analyses.
This paper is an extended version of a poster paper entitled `A life cycle assessment of grid, grid connected
PV and stand-alone PV power systems for household energy supply', by A. J. Johnson, M. E. Watt, M.
Ellis and H. R. Outhred, presented at the 14th European Photovoltaic Solar Energy Conference and
Exhibition in Barcelona, June/July 1997, for which joint copyright lies with the authors and the
conference organizers WIP. This paper was prepared with funding assistance from the Australian IEA PV
Power Systems Consortium. The ®nancial calculations were undertaken by Dorothy Remmer, using the
